channel_name: This Week in Startups · Jason Calacanis
video_title: The hottest running app has nothing to do with speed | E2303
gist: Two founder interviews: Interval (gamified territory-capture running app built lean with AI-era tooling, ~1M downloads, profitable on $60/yr subs and $12 trial CAC) and Verge Labs (formerly Verge Genomics), a neuro drug-discovery platform pivoting from developing its own drugs to selling AI 'world model' predictions to pharma, monetizing brain-tissue data moat via large milestone-laden partnerships.

themes.theme: AI-era app economics and lean teams

insights.claim: Jason Calacanis argues an app like Interval would have required ~12 people and $3-5M in seed funding 5-10 years ago, but vibe coding now makes such apps viable for tiny teams.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Lower build costs compress seed round sizes and shift consumer-app investing toward capital-efficient, profitability-first companies.
insights.quote: This app would take a company of 12 people, but 5 or 10 years ago... you got to raise about 3 to 5 million dollars to do this.

insights.claim: Louie Phillips says Interval was built by a five-person team (three developers), reached profitability before raising, grew to ~1M downloads and ~100K Instagram followers via organic social with no paid media.
insights.speaker: Louie Phillips
insights.so_what: Demonstrates a capital-efficient consumer playbook where founder-led organic content substitutes for venture-funded user acquisition.
insights.quote: we grew that to about a million downloads and about 100,000 followers on Instagram.

insights.claim: Interval's paid acquisition shows ~$12 cost per trial start on Meta against a $60/yr subscription and ~17-month average customer lifetime.
insights.speaker: Louie Phillips
insights.so_what: Roughly 5:1 LTV-to-trial-cost economics signal a scalable subscription consumer app, the unit-economics bar investors look for.
insights.quote: the cost per per trial start for us currently is about $12 um on meta... average customer lifetime is about 17 months.

insights.claim: Calacanis lays out a return-on-ad-spend model: if one in five trials convert, $12 CAC scales to the $60 sub, plus referral and lifetime-extension factors compound the payback.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Provides a reusable ROAS/LTV mental model for evaluating any subscription consumer app's flywheel.
insights.quote: if you get one in five people to convert... five times 12 you hit that $60... they make you $90 or $85

insights.claim: Calacanis notes subscription consumer apps are hit-or-miss: Calm and Fitbod made the ad flywheel work while Tone Base and especially Steezy (dance) did not.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Category and content type heavily determine whether paid acquisition flywheels work, a key diligence variable for consumer subscription bets.
insights.quote: Tone Base, Steezy didn't work out exactly... that was a harder one to make work, dance.

insights.claim: Interval gamifies running as a global 'turf wars' map where users claim territory by running a closed loop, and stolen-territory notifications drive re-engagement.
insights.speaker: Louie Phillips
insights.so_what: Personalized loss-aversion notifications are a repeatable retention lever for activity apps, relevant to the broader engagement-mechanics playbook.
insights.quote: People are just so much more motivated to go out and do that activity when they get a notification that their territory has just been stolen.

insights.claim: Louie Phillips deliberately excluded speed from Interval so casual users (even a walking grandmother) can compete, broadening the addressable market beyond elite runners who dominate Strava.
insights.speaker: Louie Phillips
insights.so_what: De-emphasizing performance widens TAM and differentiates from Strava's Olympian-skewed competitive dynamics.
insights.quote: Interval is not based on speed... it just brings in this element of anyone can compete against anyone.

insights.claim: Calacanis frames Interval as 'gamification for good,' using slot-machine app mechanics to drive healthy behavior without the red-light-running speed dangers that have caused injuries and a death tied to Strava.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Safety positioning vs Strava is a defensible brand and regulatory differentiator for activity apps.
insights.quote: you're taking the slot machine nature of apps and smartphones and you're using it for good

insights.claim: Calacanis warns gamified location apps burn out (citing Foursquare 'mayor' and Pokemon Go fatigue) and must continually refresh mechanics to sustain engagement.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Novelty decay is the core risk for location-gamification apps; durable retention features are the diligence question.
insights.quote: everybody I knew was like obsessed with becoming the mayor of their favorite sandwich shop... and then it sort of burned out.

insights.claim: Interval plans daily-reset 'arenas' (speed-based contests for hot spots like Central Park) layered on top of the volume-based territory map to solve the ephemerality and pace problems.
insights.speaker: Louie Phillips
insights.so_what: Mode-segmentation (volume vs speed) is a roadmap pattern for sustaining engagement across user types.
insights.quote: something called arenas where to capture a certain really active spot... you need to be the fastest around that spot on that day

insights.claim: Calacanis suggests expanding territory-capture mechanics to skiing, biking, and kayaking using completeness/percentage metrics rather than speed to avoid the death-risk of speed competition.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Platform expansion across activities is the path from single-app to category platform for territory-gamification.
insights.quote: a really interesting way for you to expand this would be to do say skiing or biking or kayaking... let people claim the water the mountain

themes.theme: Founder-led marketing and paid social tactics

insights.claim: Louie Phillips says founders, especially in Australia, fail because they won't 'suck publicly'; willingness to look foolish on camera was the biggest driver of Interval's organic growth.
insights.speaker: Louie Phillips
insights.so_what: Founder comfort with public, talking-head content is a leading indicator of cheap organic distribution in consumer apps.
insights.quote: you've got to prepare to suck, and you've got to prepare to suck publicly.

insights.claim: Phillips found talking-head game-explanation videos with overhead overlays outperformed silent product footage, taking Interval to 100K followers quickly.
insights.speaker: Louie Phillips
insights.so_what: Content format (explainer vs raw footage) materially changes organic conversion for products with non-obvious mechanics.
insights.quote: I would literally jump in the studio... and explain the game with some overlays above my head. And that in itself got us to 100,000 followers

insights.claim: Phillips says paid ads added predictability to a previously volatile organic-only funnel that swung between astronomical and 'crickets' months.
insights.speaker: Louie Phillips
insights.so_what: Paid acquisition's value is variance reduction, not just volume, an underappreciated point for forecasting consumer-app growth.
insights.quote: we had months which were astronomical... versus other months which were just absolute flops... ads really iron that out for us.

insights.claim: Interval outsources paid media to agency Scale on a fee that scales with ad spend rather than building the capability in-house.
insights.speaker: Louie Phillips
insights.so_what: Spend-linked agency models let lean teams scale acquisition without internal performance-marketing headcount.
insights.quote: we're actually using a uh third-party. It's called Scale... it scales with the with the spend

insights.claim: Calacanis points to Meta's public Ad Library as a free competitive-intelligence tool to study any competitor's live creative.
insights.speaker: Jason Calacanis
insights.so_what: Free ad-transparency tooling is a standard tactic for benchmarking acquisition creative and competitor spend posture.
insights.quote: Meta has an ad library... anybody can do competitive intelligence on other people's ads.

insights.claim: Verge rebranded from Verge Genomics to Verge Labs and pivoted from developing its own drugs to selling AI predictions to pharma, i.e. 'making a better machine that sells lottery tickets' instead of scratching them.
insights.speaker: Alice Zhang
insights.so_what: Platform/tooling positioning de-risks the binary clinical-trial outcome and creates recurring B2B revenue versus single-asset biotech bets.
insights.quote: instead of just buying a lottery ticket and developing a drug ourselves, can we actually make a better machine that sells those lottery tickets?

insights.claim: Verge's most valuable problem is predicting which patients will respond to a drug, letting pharma design smaller, cheaper trials (e.g. for a phase-2 schizophrenia asset behaving differently across patients).
insights.speaker: Alice Zhang
insights.so_what: Patient-response prediction is a higher-leverage, more defensible wedge into pharma R&D than target discovery alone.
insights.quote: we can help you pick out which patients to roll in your next trial that actually respond to your drug. And let you design a much smaller and cheaper clinical trial.

insights.claim: Verge offers three engagement models: solving a specific target problem, licensing existing targets/insights, or licensing the data models directly, plus platform-as-a-service for smaller biotechs.
insights.speaker: Alice Zhang
insights.so_what: Multiple monetization surfaces (services, licensing, PaaS) diversify revenue beyond milestone-dependent partnerships.
insights.quote: Companies can work with us essentially three ways... license insights or targets... or we can license the data models directly.

insights.claim: Verge's traditional pharma deals run $25-42M upfront with milestones totaling $700-800M each, and it has done partnerships with Eli Lilly and AstraZeneca/Alexion plus unannounced funding rounds.
insights.speaker: Alice Zhang
insights.so_what: Large milestone-laden contracts validate commercial traction and explain why the company hasn't needed to raise publicly.
insights.quote: it was a 25 to 42 million up front with then milestones that total up to anywhere between 700 to 800 million dollars each.

insights.claim: In the Lilly ALS partnership, 83% of Verge's targets validated in wet-lab experiments versus Lilly's stated 20% expectation bar, and Lilly optioned two targets into its internal ALS pipeline in 2024.
insights.speaker: Alice Zhang
insights.so_what: A 4x beat on validation expectations is hard external proof of platform efficacy and a strong commercial signal for bio-AI investors.
insights.quote: we actually found at the end of that partnership that 83% of those targets actually validated in wet lab experiments.

insights.claim: Verge built a brain-data set from over 12,000 human brains and 6,000 patients sourced from 24+ tissue banks, sequencing all ~30,000 genes from DNA to RNA to protein.
insights.speaker: Alice Zhang
insights.so_what: Proprietary, hard-to-replicate ground-truth data is the core moat distinguishing Verge from AI biotechs chasing easy oncology data.
insights.quote: over 12,000 human brains and 6,000 patients... we capture the behavior of all 30,000 genes in the genome at multiple levels from the DNA to RNA to protein.

insights.claim: Zhang calls brain tissue 'the lidar of neuroscience' — the molecular ground truth needed to anchor models, analogous to how Waymo's lidar beat Tesla's camera-only approach.
insights.speaker: Alice Zhang
insights.so_what: The lidar analogy reframes data-modality choice as the decisive competitive variable in applied-AI, beyond model scale.
insights.quote: brain tissue is like the lidar of neuroscience in that it's just the molecular ground truth of disease.

insights.claim: Verge's model is a transformer (same family as ChatGPT/Claude) with per-modality encoders fused into a 512-dimensional patient vector, using contrastive multimodal learning developed only in the last ~18 months.
insights.speaker: Alice Zhang
insights.so_what: Recent transformer/contrastive advances are the technical unlock enabling bio world models, signaling timing for the category.
insights.quote: the actual model architecture is at the kind of core is a transformer in the same domain as ChatGPT, Claude, and other LLMs.

insights.claim: Zhang's contrarian take: in biology, scaling parameters matters far less than scaling the right paired data and modalities, unlike text models where bigger reliably wins.
insights.speaker: Alice Zhang
insights.so_what: Challenges the compute-scaling orthodoxy for applied-AI verticals, implying data acquisition (not GPUs) is the binding constraint in bio.
insights.quote: scale really only matters when it's pointed in the right data in the right direction.

insights.claim: Verge's model shows emergent ability to reconstruct brain activity from a blood draw alone — a task it was never trained on — via masking-based multimodal training.
insights.speaker: Alice Zhang
insights.so_what: Emergent cross-modality inference could enable cheap, non-invasive 'virtual biopsies,' a potential standalone diagnostic product.
insights.quote: our own model, with high accuracy, can actually accurately reconstruct brain activity from blood alone, and that's actually not a task that it was asked to do.

insights.claim: Zhang says Verge is seeing non-linear scaling laws in its data that increase with more samples, with compute/parameter scaling not yet even started — implying large remaining headroom.
insights.speaker: Alice Zhang
insights.so_what: Untapped scaling headroom suggests the platform's predictive accuracy could compound, strengthening the long-term moat.
insights.quote: we are seeing scaling laws in our data... they're non-linear and increase as we add samples. And we haven't even started working on scaling the compute and the parameters

themes.theme: Drug economics and the future of medicine

insights.claim: Zhang notes the average drug costs ~$5B all-in, the vast majority spent on failures because 9 of 10 attempts fail at the last and most expensive stage.
insights.speaker: Alice Zhang
insights.so_what: Reducing late-stage failure even modestly is the largest cost lever in pharma, defining the value pool bio-AI is targeting.
insights.quote: it costs $5 billion on average, all in, to develop a single drug... nine out of the 10 attempts fail at the last stage and the most expensive stage.

insights.claim: Zhang predicts accurate prediction of which drugs succeed could cut all-in development cost from $5B to tens of millions, driving order-of-magnitude reductions in consumer drug prices.
insights.speaker: Alice Zhang
insights.so_what: If realized, this reprices the entire pharma cost structure and is the macro thesis underpinning bio-AI valuations.
insights.quote: it goes from $5 billion to really, you know, tens tens of millions to really get a drug all the way through.

insights.claim: Zhang predicts a 5-year shift to true personalized medicine, reframing Alzheimer's as hundreds of distinct diseases matched to specific therapies, with continuous health-state monitoring and early intervention.
insights.speaker: Alice Zhang
insights.so_what: Disease subtyping plus monitoring expands addressable markets toward prevention and longevity, not just treatment.
insights.quote: we're no longer just thinking of Alzheimer's disease as one disease, but... hundreds of diseases

insights.claim: Zhang argues neuroscience drug discovery lags cancer by 10-20 years because the brain can only be tissue-sampled from deceased patients, and Alzheimer's/dementia will be a top mortality driver as populations age.
insights.speaker: Alice Zhang
insights.so_what: The access difficulty that holds neuro back is precisely the barrier-to-entry that makes Verge's solved pipeline valuable.
insights.quote: neuroscience is long behind cancer by 10-20 years... It will really be Alzheimer's disease and dementias.

insights.claim: Zhang warns the biggest risk to bio-AI is human impatience — generalizing one clinical-trial setback into 'AI doesn't work' — and stresses Verge published its failed trial data and fed it back into the platform.
insights.speaker: Alice Zhang
insights.so_what: Sentiment-driven retreat after early failures is a market-timing risk for bio-AI investors, not a technical one.
insights.quote: the biggest risk is more of a a like a human one, which is that we kind of lose interest in AI or in the application of AI in healthcare just because we kind of face one setback

insights.claim: Zhang says talent is hardest at the intersection of frontier-AI and biology, but mission alignment (often a personal disease experience) makes these 'unicorns' easy to recruit and shields Verge from competing head-on with labs like Anthropic.
insights.speaker: Alice Zhang
insights.so_what: Mission-differentiated hiring is a non-obvious competitive advantage in the brutal AI talent market.
insights.quote: it's actually about finding those people that that have both.
